title: Detecting and Removing Duplicates from a Linked List

statement.markdown: You are given a singly linked list and a function that needs to be used to remove duplicate nodes from the list while preserving the original order of nodes. The function is a callback function that takes the value of a node as an argument and returns a boolean indicating whether the node should be removed or not. The function should be called on each node in the list and if it returns true, the node should be deleted. The function should be called for only the first occurrence of each node value.
statement.inputFormat: The function will be called with a singly linked list as input.
statement.outputFormat: The modified linked list with duplicates removed.
statement.notes: The function should be called for only the first occurrence of each node value.

examples.explanation: The function will be called on each node in the list and if it returns true, the node will be deleted. The function will be called for only the first occurrence of each node value.
The function will be called on each node in the list and if it returns true, the node will be deleted. The function will be called for only the first occurrence of each node value.

hints.text: You should iterate through the list only once to avoid visiting nodes twice.
Use a set to store the values of the nodes that have been seen so far.

editorial.title: Detecting and Removing Duplicates from a Linked List
editorial.intuition: We can use a set to store the values of the nodes that have been seen so far. We then iterate through the list only once and call the function on each node. If the function returns true, we delete the node. We do this by updating the next pointer of the previous node to skip the current node.
editorial.bruteForce: We can iterate through the list twice. The first time, we check if a node is a duplicate by calling the function. The second time, we delete the nodes that are duplicates.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a set to store the values of the nodes that have been seen so far and iterate through the list only once.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a set to store the values of the nodes that have been seen so far, iterate through the list only once, and update the next pointer of the previous node to skip the current node if it is a duplicate.
commonMistakes: Not using a set to store node values

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def should_remove(self, x):
        seen = set()
        while x in seen:
            x = x.next
        seen.add(x.val)
        return x is not None

    def remove_duplicates(self, head):
        current = head
        while current:
            next_node = current.next
            if self.should_remove(next_node):
                # Delete next node
                current.next = next_node.next
            else:
                current = current.next
        return head

# Example usage
